era_context: The Late Hellenistic period is entering a phase of major conflict. The Fifth Syrian War (202-195 BCE) has recently concluded with Seleucid victory and Egyptian weakness. Antiochus III is at the height of his power and is now turning his attention westward, toward Asia Minor and Greece. Rome is consolidating its dominance in the Mediterranean. The Aetolian League in Greece is seeking allies against Roman expansion. China under Emperor Wen continues a period of stability and economic growth.

events.title: Outbreak of the Syrian War (Antiochus War); Antiochus III invades Greece
events.region: Greece and Asia Minor (Seleucid Empire vs. Rome)
events.description: Antiochus III, encouraged by the Aetolian League and other Greek states seeking to resist Roman expansion, invades Greece with a large army. This marks the beginning of the Syrian War (192-188 BCE), a major conflict between the Seleucid Empire and Rome. Antiochus lands in Thessaly with approximately 10,000 troops and begins operations against Roman-allied forces. The invasion is initially successful, but it will ultimately lead to Antiochus's defeat and the decline of Seleucid power in the Mediterranean. The war is a turning point in Hellenistic history, establishing Rome as the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: The outbreak of the Syrian War in 192 BCE is well-attested in multiple independent sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: -188-001 (Peace of Apamea, end of Syrian War)

events.title: Battle of Thermopylae; Roman victory over Antiochus III
events.description: Roman forces under Manius Acilius Glabrio defeat Antiochus III at the Battle of Thermopylae. Antiochus, despite his numerical superiority, is outmaneuvered by the Romans, who use superior tactics and knowledge of the terrain. The battle is a decisive Roman victory and marks a turning point in the Syrian War. Antiochus is forced to retreat from Greece, and his invasion of the Balkans is effectively ended. The battle demonstrates Roman military superiority and the decline of Hellenistic military power.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Thermopylae in 192 BCE is well-attested in multiple sources. The date is secure. Ancient sources provide detailed accounts of the battle, though some details are disputed.

events.title: Antiochus III retreats from Greece; returns to Asia Minor
events.description: Following his defeat at Thermopylae, Antiochus III withdraws his forces from Greece and returns to Asia Minor. The retreat marks the end of Seleucid hopes for dominance in the Balkans and Greece. Antiochus will continue to fight the Romans in Asia Minor, but the strategic initiative has passed to Rome. The retreat is a significant moment in the decline of Hellenistic power and the rise of Roman dominance.
events.certainty_note: The retreat of Antiochus III from Greece is well-attested in multiple sources. The date is secure within ±1 year.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the Battle of Thermopylae in the Syrian War is sometimes confused with the famous Battle of Thermopylae (480 BCE) against the Persians. Some sources provide slightly different casualty figures and tactical details. However, the overall narrative of the battle and its significance is well-established. The date of 192 BCE is secure.
historiographic_note: The Syrian War is one of the best-documented events of the Hellenistic period, with detailed accounts from multiple sources, particularly Livy and Polybius. However, the sources are heavily weighted toward the Roman perspective. Seleucid and Greek viewpoints are underrepresented. The dating of events is generally reliable to within ±1 year. The war marks a major turning point in Mediterranean history and is extensively studied by modern scholars.

graph_edges.note: The invasion of Greece led directly to the Battle of Thermopylae
The Roman victory at Thermopylae forced Antiochus to retreat from Greece
The Syrian War that began in 192 BCE ended with the Peace of Apamea in 188 BCE
